chunks.title: Variables and types
chunks.text: A variable is a named reference to a value stored in memory. Statically typed languages (Java, C++, Rust) check types at compile time — every variable has a declared type. Dynamically typed languages (Python, JavaScript, Ruby) check types at runtime. Primitive types include integers, floats, booleans, characters; composite types include arrays, strings, structs, objects. Mutability matters: a constant or immutable value cannot be reassigned.

chunks.title: Control flow
chunks.text: Control flow determines the order in which statements run. if/else branches on a condition. switch/match selects one of several arms. while loops repeat until a condition becomes false; for loops iterate a fixed number of times or over a sequence. break exits a loop early; continue skips to the next iteration. Early return from a function makes code flatter than deep nesting. Exception handling (try/catch/finally) handles error paths separately from happy paths.

chunks.title: Functions
chunks.text: A function takes arguments and returns a value. It encapsulates a reusable piece of logic. Parameters can have defaults (lang='en') and accept variable arity (*args, **kwargs in Python). First-class functions can be passed as arguments, returned from other functions, and stored in variables — the basis of higher-order functions like map, filter, reduce. Pure functions have no side effects and always return the same output for the same input; they're easier to reason about and test.

chunks.title: Object-oriented programming
chunks.text: OOP organizes code around objects that bundle data (attributes) and behaviour (methods). Classes define the shape; instances are concrete objects. Encapsulation hides internal state behind methods. Inheritance lets a subclass extend a parent class. Polymorphism allows different classes to be used interchangeably through a common interface. Composition (an object holding other objects) is often a better tool than inheritance for code reuse.

chunks.title: Recursion and iteration
chunks.text: Iteration uses loops; recursion has a function call itself on a smaller subproblem. Every recursion needs a base case to terminate. Recursion is natural for tree/graph traversal and divide-and-conquer; iteration is usually faster and uses constant stack space. Many languages can't optimize tail calls — deep recursion overflows the call stack. Converting recursion to iteration usually involves managing an explicit stack data structure.

chunks.title: Exceptions and error handling
chunks.text: Exceptions signal that something unexpected happened. Code that might fail is wrapped in try; specific failures are caught in catch/except blocks; finally runs no matter what. Exceptions should be used for exceptional conditions, not control flow. Wrapping low-level exceptions in domain-specific ones gives callers a stable contract. Languages like Rust and Go avoid exceptions, returning Result/error values that callers must handle explicitly.

chunks.title: Memory and references
chunks.text: In low-level languages (C, C++), the programmer manages memory directly — malloc/new allocates, free/delete returns memory. Forgetting to free leaks memory; using freed memory causes use-after-free bugs. Higher-level languages use garbage collection (Java, Python, Go) to free unreachable objects automatically. Rust uses ownership and borrowing: every value has exactly one owner; references are checked at compile time to prevent dangling pointers without GC.

chunks.title: Time and space complexity
chunks.text: Time complexity is how runtime grows with input size; space complexity is how memory does. Big-O notation describes the dominant term as input grows. O(1) is constant; O(log n) very fast; O(n) linear; O(n log n) typical of efficient sorts; O(n²) starts to hurt; O(2^n) impractical. Always profile before optimizing — a O(n) algorithm in a hot loop can dominate a O(n²) one called rarely. Premature optimization is the root of much wasted effort.

chunks.title: Closures
chunks.text: A closure is a function plus the captured environment of variables from its enclosing scope. The captured variables stay alive as long as the closure exists, even after the enclosing function returns. Closures power higher-order functions, callbacks, partial application, and decorators. Beware capturing mutable state — many off-by-one bugs come from a closure capturing a loop variable by reference instead of by value.

chunks.title: Lambdas and anonymous functions
chunks.text: Lambdas (anonymous functions) are functions defined inline without a name. JavaScript's arrow functions, Python's lambda x: x+1, Java's x -> x + 1. They're useful for short callbacks and higher-order function arguments (map, filter, sort comparator). Restricted in some languages — Python lambdas are limited to a single expression. Long inline logic should usually be a named function for readability.

chunks.title: Currying and partial application
chunks.text: Currying transforms a function of multiple arguments into a chain of functions each taking one: f(a, b, c) becomes f(a)(b)(c). Partial application fixes some arguments to produce a function of the remaining ones. Useful for creating specialized versions of general functions, for function composition, and for working with point-free style. Built into Haskell; explicit (functools.partial) in Python.

chunks.title: Generators and iterators
chunks.text: An iterator produces a sequence of values on demand via next() / hasNext(). A generator is a function that yields values one at a time, pausing between yields — its state is preserved across yields. Python's yield, JS's function*. Generators enable lazy infinite sequences and decouple producers from consumers. Memory-efficient: process one item at a time instead of materializing the whole sequence.

chunks.title: Lazy evaluation
chunks.text: Lazy evaluation defers computing a value until it's needed. Haskell evaluates lazily by default. Python iterators and generators are lazy. Java streams are lazy until a terminal operation. Benefits: infinite sequences, short-circuit on first match, avoid wasted work. Pitfalls: harder to reason about when work actually happens, can defer errors to surprising locations, memory leaks if accumulated computations are kept alive.

chunks.title: Functional programming concepts
chunks.text: Functional programming emphasises pure functions, immutability, and first-class functions. Map transforms each element; filter keeps elements matching a predicate; reduce combines them. Function composition (f ∘ g)(x) = f(g(x)) builds complex transformations from simple ones. Pure FP languages (Haskell) push side effects into explicit constructs (monads). Multi-paradigm languages (Python, JS, Kotlin, Scala) borrow FP techniques where helpful.

chunks.title: Pattern matching
chunks.text: Pattern matching destructures values and dispatches based on their shape. Rust's match, Scala's match, Python 3.10+'s match. Patterns can match literals, types, tuples, records, list shapes (head/tail), with guards (if conditions). More expressive than switch statements and exhaustive (the compiler warns when not all cases are handled). Central to algebraic data types and Result-style error handling.

chunks.title: Algebraic data types
chunks.text: Algebraic data types combine product types (records: this AND that) and sum types (unions: this OR that). Rust's enum, Haskell's data, TypeScript's discriminated unions. Pattern matching is the natural way to consume them. They eliminate whole classes of bugs from sentinel values, nulls, and untyped flags. Option<T> and Result<T, E> are canonical examples — explicit about presence and error.

chunks.title: Immutability
chunks.text: Immutable values can't be modified after creation; modifications return new values. Eliminates a huge class of bugs around shared state. Easier to reason about, easier to parallelize. Trade-off: copying can be expensive (mitigated by persistent data structures with structural sharing). Modern languages: Rust's let by default is immutable, JavaScript's const for binding (not values), Java's final, Python's tuples and frozenset.

chunks.title: Side effects vs pure functions
chunks.text: A side effect is anything beyond returning a value — modifying state, I/O, throwing exceptions, calling external services. Pure functions have no side effects: same input always returns the same output, no observable changes. Pure functions are trivially testable, parallelizable, memoizable, and composable. Real programs need side effects; functional design pushes them to the edges and keeps the core pure.

chunks.title: Modules and packages
chunks.text: Modules group related code into a separate compilation unit with explicit imports/exports. Packages are collections of modules distributed together. Each language has its package manager: npm (JS), pip/poetry (Python), cargo (Rust), Maven (Java), go modules (Go). Lock files pin exact versions for reproducible builds. Semantic versioning lets package managers update non-breaking changes automatically.

chunks.title: Namespaces
chunks.text: A namespace prevents naming collisions by scoping identifiers — std::vector vs MyLib::vector. Implementations vary: explicit namespaces (C++ namespace), modules (Python, Rust), files (Go where the file path determines the import path). Without namespaces, large codebases collide constantly. Star imports (import * from x) defeat namespacing and create confusion; prefer explicit imports.

chunks.title: Static vs instance methods
chunks.text: An instance method operates on a specific object — it has access to that object's state via this/self. A static method (Java) or class method (Python @staticmethod) belongs to the class itself, with no instance. Static methods are useful for factory methods, utility functions, and constants. Overusing static methods often signals procedural code masquerading as OOP — consider top-level functions instead.

chunks.title: Abstract classes vs interfaces
chunks.text: An interface declares method signatures without implementation. Classes implementing it must provide all methods. Multiple interfaces can be implemented. An abstract class can have both abstract (must override) and concrete (default) methods, plus state. Only one abstract class can be inherited (in single-inheritance languages). Modern Java and C# blur the distinction with default interface methods.

chunks.title: Generics and parametric polymorphism
chunks.text: Generics let you write code that works for any type while preserving type safety. List<T> works for List<Integer> or List<String>. Each instantiation gets type checked. Generic constraints (T extends Comparable<T>) restrict what types can be used. Implementations: type erasure (Java, runtime sees Object) or monomorphization (Rust, C++ templates, compile-time specialization for each type — fast but bloats binary).

chunks.title: Concurrency primitives
chunks.text: Concurrency primitives coordinate work across threads or async tasks. Atomic operations (CompareAndSwap) provide lock-free updates of single words. Mutexes serialise access to shared data. Channels pass messages between concurrent units (Go, Rust). Semaphores limit concurrent access to a resource. Read-write locks let many readers or one writer. Async/await composes futures without explicit threads. Pick the right primitive for the synchronization pattern.

chunks.title: Threads, coroutines, goroutines
chunks.text: OS threads are preemptively scheduled by the kernel — heavyweight (megabytes of stack), context switches cost microseconds. Green threads / fibers / coroutines run in user space, cooperatively scheduled, lightweight (kilobytes). Go's goroutines start at 8KB and grow on demand, multiplexed onto OS threads by the runtime. Python's asyncio coroutines run on one thread; threading uses OS threads (GIL-limited for CPU work). Match the model to the workload.

chunks.title: Promises and async/await
chunks.text: A promise (or future) represents a value that may not be ready yet, with then/catch handlers for success/failure. async/await is syntactic sugar: writing await expr suspends the function until expr resolves, then resumes — looks synchronous but doesn't block the thread. Pitfalls: forgetting await leaves dangling promises, async functions return promises (callers must handle them), parallelism requires Promise.all not sequential awaits.

chunks.title: Reactive programming
chunks.text: Reactive programming models data as streams of values over time. Observables emit values; observers subscribe and react. Operators compose streams — map, filter, debounce, merge. RxJS, Reactor, Rx.NET. Great for UI events, async data flows, complex event processing. Trade-off: steep learning curve, debugging stack traces is harder, easy to write subtle memory leaks if subscriptions aren't disposed.

chunks.title: Event-driven programming
chunks.text: Event-driven programs respond to events (user clicks, network responses, timers) rather than following a top-down sequence. The runtime maintains an event loop, polling for events and dispatching to handlers. JavaScript in the browser, Node.js, GUI frameworks (GTK, Cocoa), game engines (Unity, Godot). Combines well with non-blocking I/O — one thread handles thousands of connections.

chunks.title: Memoization
chunks.text: Memoization caches function results by their arguments so the second call returns instantly. Trade space for time. Especially useful for pure functions with overlapping calls — Fibonacci recursion blows up from O(2^n) to O(n) with memoization. Implementations: decorator (Python @lru_cache), library helper (lodash memoize), hand-rolled dict. Beware: caches need eviction strategy, mutable arguments break caching, side-effectful functions are unsafe to memoize.

chunks.title: Higher-order functions
chunks.text: A higher-order function takes a function as argument or returns one. map(fn, list), filter(predicate, list), sort(comparator), reduce(combiner, init, list). They abstract over the loop structure, letting you focus on the per-element logic. Returning a function enables decorators, currying, and dependency injection. Languages without first-class functions (early Java) needed bulky interface workarounds; modern languages support them directly.

chunks.title: Tail call optimization
chunks.text: A tail call is one that's the last action of a function. With TCO, the compiler reuses the current stack frame for the call instead of pushing a new one — turning recursion into iteration in terms of stack usage. Required in Scheme, supported in many functional languages, opportunistic in C++ and others, absent from Python and standard JavaScript. Without TCO, deeply recursive code overflows the stack.

chunks.title: Pass by value vs pass by reference
chunks.text: Pass by value copies the argument; the callee can't affect the caller's variable. Pass by reference passes a pointer/reference; modifications affect the caller. C passes everything by value (but pointers are values you can dereference). Java passes object references by value — you can mutate the object but can't reassign the caller's variable. Python passes references by value too, with the same caveat.

chunks.title: Iterators and iteration protocol
chunks.text: An iterator is an object that knows how to traverse a collection one element at a time. Python: __iter__ returns self, __next__ returns next value or raises StopIteration. JavaScript: [Symbol.iterator]() returns {next() -> {value, done}}. for-each loops are usually sugar for the iterator protocol. Custom iterators let any object work with for loops, list comprehensions, map/filter/reduce.

chunks.title: Naming things
chunks.text: Phil Karlton: 'There are only two hard things in computer science: cache invalidation and naming things.' Good names reveal intent without comments. Bad names force readers to trace usage to understand. Conventions: nouns for variables and types, verbs for functions, boolean prefixes (is, has, can). Length should match scope — short loop counters fine, longer for module-level constants. Renaming is a recurring refactoring activity, not a one-time cost.
